content: Beginners often attribute rich interview results to luck rather than skill, assuming they 'found a good one' rather than recognizing their own effective behaviors. This attribution error prevents them from learning and repeating what actually worked. Unpacking the specific, repeatable behaviors that led to great insights is far more valuable than chalking it up to chance.
original_quote: When students come back with great material, it's not useful (to them, or to the class) to assume they got lucky; but it's incredibly valuable to unpack what they did (specific, repeatable behaviors they engaged) to discover such interesting material.
implication: Leaders and coaches should resist letting teams dismiss their wins as luck. Debriefing what specific behaviors generated great outcomes builds repeatable craft and sustainable confidence.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Great empathy interviews are the product of craft, not magic or lucky subject selection. Observers watching skilled designers often mistake interviewing mastery for an unusually interesting subject. The quality of the output reflects the quality of the interviewer's technique.
original_quote: The reality is, it's not magic: it's craft. And the craft can be learned.
implication: Organizations investing in user research or customer discovery should treat interviewing as a trainable, improvable skill — not a talent some people have and others don't.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Utley challenges students to treat finding what makes their conversation partner fascinating as a personal challenge, reframing the task from passive luck to active discovery. This mindset shift turns any assigned customer into a puzzle worth solving. The goal is to discover why your interviewee is the most interesting person in the room.
original_quote: Your goal is to discover why you're the luckiest team in the room, why your customer is the most interesting customer you could have been randomly assigned. Take it as a personal challenge to discover what makes your conversation partner the most fascinating person in the room.
implication: For researchers, designers, and leaders conducting conversations, reframing curiosity as a competitive challenge — rather than hoping for a 'good' subject — unlocks richer insights from any interaction.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Utley uses the metaphor of Michelangelo's 'angel in the marble' to describe what skilled interviewers do: the fascinating story already exists inside the person, and the interviewer's job is to carve a conversation that reveals it. The insight is latent; the craft is in the excavation.
original_quote: There's an angel in that marble, you've just got to patiently carve a conversation that reveals its contours.
implication: Practitioners of user research or leadership conversations should approach every dialogue as an act of patient excavation — assuming richness exists and their role is to surface it, not to hope it appears.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: A practical way to build interviewing and curiosity skills is to treat everyday social encounters — dinner parties, work functions — as low-stakes practice grounds. By actively trying to discover what obscure topic someone is a world-expert in, you train the habit of genuine curiosity. Everyone knows something deeply that most others don't.
original_quote: Everyone is a world-expert in something - meaning, they know more about some obscure topic, subject, or skill more than 95% of people. Make a game with yourself to discover what that is, and then allow them to carry the conversation in an unexpected direction, according to their passion.
implication: Curiosity is a muscle built through daily informal reps, not just formal research sessions. Leaders and innovators who practice it socially arrive to professional contexts with far sharper listening instincts.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Utley endorses Paul Graham's practice of deliberately seeking out what unusual experts know that others don't — and doing so out of genuine interest, not conversational strategy. The distinction matters: authentic curiosity produces better conversations than performed interest. Graham's framing reinforces that this practice is about real learning, not social maneuvering.
original_quote: When I meet someone who knows a lot about something unusual (which includes practically everyone, if you dig deep enough), I try to learn what they know that other people don't. There are almost always surprises here... I really want to know.
implication: For innovators and leaders, developing genuine intellectual curiosity — not just conversational technique — is the foundational skill that makes learning from anyone possible and sustainable.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Genuine interest is the most effective and sustainable way to connect with and learn from others. Because anyone can be genuinely interesting if you look deeply enough, you can authentically engage with virtually anyone you encounter. This creates a virtuous cycle: curiosity enables learning, and learning justifies more curiosity.
original_quote: Genuine interest is always the best cover, and because you can learn from anyone, you can be genuinely interested in anyone you end up meeting.
implication: Rather than faking curiosity as a rapport-building tactic, innovators should cultivate real curiosity as a worldview — trusting that depth exists in every person, and that finding it is worth the effort.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Seemingly irrelevant information encountered through curious conversation can prove unexpectedly valuable later. Utley sees this as a reason to stay open and curious even when a topic feels tangential or unrelated to your current work. Cross-domain inputs often become the seeds of breakthrough ideas.
original_quote: You never know when a seemingly irrelevant piece of information will come in handy.
implication: Builders and creators should resist filtering conversations for immediate utility. Absorbing broadly — and trusting that unexpected connections emerge over time — is a key driver of creative insight.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Utley opens the essay with a classic creative writing exercise: study an ordinary cab driver until you can describe him as utterly unique and individual. This frames the core skill of the piece — specificity in observation — as the foundation of both great writing and great design research.
original_quote: 'Go out into the streets of Paris,' he was told by an older writer, 'and pick out a cab driver. He will look to you very much like every other cab driver. But study him until you can describe him so that he is seen in your description to be an individual, different from every other cab driver in the world.'
implication: The discipline of seeing individuals as individuals — not as archetypes or categories — is central to both creative craft and human-centered design. Training this observational specificity sharpens empathy and insight.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Accomplished beginners often exhibit an 'unhelpful humility' — they produce great work but attribute it to external luck rather than their own capability. This pattern is especially common in early design work when students return from fieldwork with unexpectedly rich material. The humility, while seemingly virtuous, actually blocks learning and skill development.
original_quote: I've noticed an unhelpful humility often accompanies good design work, especially when accomplished beginners. Countless times, when students come back with particularly rich material from the field, they seem even more surprised than the rest of the class: 'I guess we just found a good one...'
implication: Educators, coaches, and team leads should actively counter false modesty in high performers. Helping people own their process — not just their outcomes — accelerates skill development and creative confidence.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Letting conversation partners lead the discussion in unexpected directions — following their passion rather than a fixed agenda — produces richer and more surprising insights. Utley frames this as a deliberate practice: discover their expertise, then let them run with it. Relinquishing conversational control is itself a skill.
original_quote: Make a game with yourself to discover what that is, and then allow them to carry the conversation in an unexpected direction, according to their passion.
implication: In interviews, leadership conversations, or creative collaborations, the instinct to stay 'on script' often limits discovery. Practiced interviewers learn when to release control and follow unexpected threads.
source_ref: How To Learn From Anyone

content: Creativity is not a rare gift reserved for a select few — it is an inherent human quality that everyone possesses. Like any skill or muscle, it can be developed, strengthened, and refined through deliberate practice. The difference between creative and uncreative people is not innate talent but whether they exercise their creative faculties.
original_quote: Creativity is an inherent human quality, which everyone shares. But some people practice using their creative faculties, while others do not. It's just like any other skill or muscle. It can be developed, strengthened, and refined through practice.
implication: Leaders and teams should stop gatekeeping creative roles to 'creative types' and instead treat creativity as a trainable organizational capability that compounds with intentional practice.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: A seventh-grader's definition captures the cognitive science of creativity precisely: 'Creativity is doing more than the first thing you think of.' Humans suffer from a well-documented 'first idea bias' — once a solution appears, we tend to stop thinking. Research shows that if you generate 10 solutions, the first is very unlikely to be the best.
original_quote: Creativity is doing more than the first thing you think of... There's a well-documented human tendency called the 'first idea bias,' or what some refer to as 'functional fixedness' or 'cognitive closure.' It means that, once we come up with some sort of solution, we often stop thinking. But there's no reason to believe the first solution is the best one. In fact, research shows that, if you think of 10 different solutions, the first is very unlikely to be the best.
implication: Any creative or problem-solving process should be deliberately structured to push past the first idea. Building in a rule of 'generate at least 10 options before evaluating' can dramatically improve outcome quality.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: AI excels precisely where human cognition is weakest: generating alternatives beyond the first idea. Unlike humans, for whom ideation consumes energy, AI can produce alternatives effortlessly using analogy, lateral thinking, and combinatorial association. This makes AI a powerful antidote to first-idea bias.
original_quote: If creativity is doing more than the first thing you think of, then it can push you to explore further. You can ask it, 'Give me 10 other ideas,' and it will keep going. Unlike humans, for whom ideation consumes energy, AI can generate alternatives effortlessly and quickly using analogy, lateral thinking, or combinatorial association. That's creative by definition.
implication: Teams should use AI not just to execute tasks faster but specifically to break out of cognitive closure — prompting it explicitly for divergent alternatives whenever they feel satisfied with an early solution.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: AI can help people reach 'good enough' ideas quickly, but that is about efficiency, not creativity. The creative potential of AI is largely unrealized because humans let cognitive biases — especially the tendency to stop at 'good enough' — prevent them from pushing further. Unlocking true creativity requires the human to consciously demand more from the model.
original_quote: AI often helps people get to 'good enough' ideas very quickly. But that's more about efficiency than creativity. Unless the human consciously pushes the model for more, they won't unlock true creativity.
implication: The bottleneck in AI-augmented creative work is human behavior, not AI capability. Building explicit prompting habits that push past the first satisfactory output is the critical skill to develop.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: AI functions like a mirror: it reflects and amplifies what the user brings to it. An HR expert who approaches a model with expert-level thinking will get expert-level output; a novice asking the same question will get far less. The model's power lies in matching and extending the user's existing knowledge, not replacing their thinking.
original_quote: The AI model reflects what you bring to it. If you're an HR expert, and you approach the model with expert-level thinking, you'll get an expert response... AI reveals things to you it won't reveal to someone else. That's why I say the model's power is in its ability to match and extend the user's thinking. It doesn't replace your creativity, it amplifies it.
implication: Investing in your own domain expertise and self-awareness remains essential in the AI era — the quality of AI output is a direct function of the quality of knowledge and context you bring to the conversation.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: The single biggest mistake people make with AI is providing insufficient context. Most people write five-word prompts — essentially Google searches — and are disappointed by the output. Utley recommends never prompting a model without at least 400 words of context, treating the AI like a new team member who needs full onboarding, not a search engine.
original_quote: I would recommend never prompting a model without at least 400 words of context. Most people say, 'My average prompt is five words.' Well, that's a Google search... If you want to work with an LLM, treat it like you would treat a new team member. Imagine if we were starting a job together and I turned to you and said, 'Make social media strategy now.' That's five words. And it's absurd, right? We would never treat a human colleague that way. But we do that with AI all the time.
implication: Before prompting AI for creative or strategic work, invest time establishing context: brand voice, objectives, tone, market, constraints. The depth of your setup directly determines the usefulness of what you get back.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: Treating AI as a teammate rather than a tool is a mindset shift with practical consequences. The way you engage with a teammate — with curiosity, respect, and full presence — produces better outputs than treating it as a passive instrument. Utley argues that how you approach the model reflects your own humanity, and that quality of presence influences quality of output.
original_quote: Teammate, without a doubt. It's about mindset. The way you treat a tool is fundamentally different from the way you treat a teammate... Bring your full self, your curiosity, your respect. That's the teammate approach. And it gets better results.
implication: Organizations should deliberately cultivate a 'teammate' mental model for AI adoption — not because AI has feelings, but because the collaborative mindset changes user behavior in ways that produce measurably better outcomes.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: A practical onboarding exercise for AI adoption is to ask the model to play the role of a 'ChatGPT adviser' and have it interview you about your workflows, responsibilities, and KPIs before making recommendations. This prompt is role-independent — it works for interns, managers, and executives alike — and creates a 'double delivery': it solves your immediate need while opening the door to deeper AI fluency.
original_quote: 'Hi, ChatGPT. I'd like you to play the role of a ChatGPT adviser. I'm looking for ways to use ChatGPT in my work. Would you, as a ChatGPT adviser, please interview me about my workflows, responsibilities, and KPIs until you have enough information to make two obvious and two non-obvious recommendations. Go ahead and begin the interview now.'... I like to call it a 'double delivery' exercise. It delivers on your current objective, and it opens the door to deeper exploration.
implication: This prompt is a low-barrier, high-value entry point for anyone struggling to integrate AI into daily work. Leaders can share it org-wide as a concrete first step toward AI fluency without requiring training programs.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: Unlike static software tools like Excel or PowerPoint that cannot teach you how to use them, AI can teach you how to use AI. This creates a fundamentally new learning paradigm where the tool itself becomes the instructor, compressing the time between adoption and fluency.
original_quote: Unlike tools such as Excel or PowerPoint, which can't teach you how to use them, AI can teach you how to use AI. That paradigm shifts how you think about learning. You're not just using it to complete tasks. You're also discovering how to partner with it.
implication: Organizations no longer need to wait for formal AI training programs before employees can build fluency. Encouraging people to ask AI how to use AI is itself a scalable, self-reinforcing learning strategy.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: Posing multiple questions — rather than a single question — is a foundational creative workflow principle. Many people fail to innovate because they are solving the wrong problem; asking only one question locks you in if that question is incorrect. Exploring a range of questions and generating multiple answers reduces that risk and surfaces the breadth of ideas needed for genuine innovation.
original_quote: One of the most important things is to pose more than one question. Seek more than one answer. Try more than one thing... Often, people are seeking to address the wrong problem. If you ask only one question, and it's the wrong one, you're stuck. But if you ask more than one, you minimize that risk.
implication: Creative workflows should be designed with deliberate question-divergence phases before converging on a problem definition. AI makes this low-cost — use it to rapidly explore multiple problem framings before committing to one.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: AI can serve as a creativity coach by being assigned a specific expert persona and asked to evaluate ideas and generate alternatives. This is functionally similar to hiring a negotiation coach or design strategist — the key is giving the model a defined role and asking the right questions to unlock its capabilities.
original_quote: The key is realizing that, if you give an AI model a specific persona – a creativity expert, say – and then ask it to evaluate an idea, it can provide meaningful feedback that pushes your thinking, as well as generate alternative suggestions. That's not fundamentally different from what we might expect from, say, a negotiation coach or a design strategist. It's a tool with vast capabilities – if you ask the right questions.
implication: Assigning AI a specific expert persona before asking for feedback is a simple but powerful prompting technique. Builders and leaders should experiment with role-based prompts to get more rigorous, actionable creative feedback.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: While tools and interfaces evolve, the fundamental human creative impulse has not changed. What is genuinely new is the level of uncertainty students and professionals face about the future — including uncertainty about what jobs will even exist. This requires a shift from career planning to 'creative planning under uncertainty.'
original_quote: Now, that's gone. Students say things like, 'I don't even know what jobs are going to be there.' That fundamental uncertainty is new. So, when students ask, 'How can I plan for an unstable future?' it's a completely justified question. The challenge now isn't just career planning. It's creative planning under uncertainty.
implication: Leaders developing talent strategies should move away from role-specific skill planning toward cultivating adaptive creative capacity — the ability to generate novel solutions and reframe problems in rapidly shifting contexts.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley envisions AI becoming a permanent equal partner in the ideation process — a trusted creative colleague who always surfaces new ideas, pushes thinking, and illuminates blind spots. Future creative teams will not just use AI as a utility but will integrate it as a full member of the ideation table.
original_quote: I cannot imagine a world where AI is not an equal partner in the ideation process. It's going to sit at the table as a member of creative teams. Teams will come to see it as a creative partner, a trusted colleague who always has new ideas, who pushes your thinking, who helps you see blind spots.
implication: Organizations should begin designing team structures, meeting formats, and creative rituals that formally include AI as a participant — not an afterthought — to get ahead of this shift before it becomes standard practice.
source_ref: Creativity in the Age of AI: A Conversation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy argues that innovation is a practice, not an event. Just as you wouldn't call yourself a pianist if you only took lessons as a child, you can't claim to be innovative based on a hackathon badge or a bootcamp from years ago. Consistent, regular engagement is what builds real capability.
original_quote: innovation is a practice, not an event
implication: Organizations and individuals who rely on occasional workshops or sprints as their entire innovation strategy are building false confidence. Sustained creative output requires treating innovation like a daily discipline.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy critiques the over-association of innovation with events like hackathons, bootcamps, and sprints. These events are not inherently bad, but if they are the extent of your innovation practice, you are in serious trouble. The LinkedIn badge for attending a workshop is not equivalent to actual creative capacity.
original_quote: there's this over association of innovation with events. And you know, at the d school I'm all about leveraging its brands or a hackathon or a workshop. That's fine. But if those events are the extent of your practice, you're in trouble.
implication: Leaders should stop measuring innovation health by the number of workshops run or badges earned, and start measuring it by the ongoing cadence of idea generation and problem-solving activity.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy uses the analogy of 'corporate athletes' to describe professionals who show up to high-stakes creative challenges without any recent training. They expect peak performance but have done no preparatory work, and they wonder why they fail or get injured. The gap between expectation and preparation is the core problem.
original_quote: we have a lot of corporate athletes, which is a generous term who are pulling hammy, at the local track meet. Right. Because the last time they ran a sprint was in high school.
implication: Before your next big innovation challenge — a product launch, a pivot, a strategic planning session — ask whether your team has been doing the creative equivalent of daily training, or whether they're showing up cold.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy defines innovation simply as an effective solution to a problem — not as a technology product or a startup idea. This definition deliberately decouples innovation from the tech world and makes it universally applicable across HR, finance, operations, and any domain where problems exist.
original_quote: innovation is an effective solution to a problem, full stop... A new expense reimbursement process or procedure could be an innovation, right. But it's an effective solution to a problem. That's an innovation.
implication: Every professional, regardless of industry or function, is a potential innovator. Reframing innovation as effective problem-solving removes the gatekeeping that causes non-tech workers to opt out of creative thinking.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy asserts that every problem is fundamentally an idea problem. This reframe shifts how professionals think about their effectiveness at work — the bottleneck is rarely resources or execution alone, but the quality and quantity of ideas generated in response to challenges.
original_quote: every problem is fundamentally an idea problem, it shifts your entire orientation to how you think about being effective at work.
implication: If every problem is an idea problem, then investing in your idea-generation capacity is one of the highest-leverage things a leader or entrepreneur can do. It reframes creativity not as a soft skill but as a core operational competency.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy's work at Stanford's d.school over 12 years, leading accelerators and executive programs globally, is grounded in building organizational capacity for routine innovation. His work spans Israel, Colombia, Japan, and New Zealand — suggesting that the principles of innovation practice are culturally transferable.
original_quote: It came out last fall and it's based on, you know, 12 years of leading the accelerators at the d school, leading executive programs and really seeking to build capacity in organizations all around the world and Israel and Columbia and Japan and New Zealand.
implication: Innovation as a practiced capacity is not a Silicon Valley-specific phenomenon. Leaders building teams in diverse geographic and cultural contexts can apply structured innovation frameworks with confidence in their transferability.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy positions creativity and innovation as capacities that can be built, nurtured, grown, and attended to regularly — not fixed traits you either have or don't. This is a direct challenge to the common belief that creative people are born, not made.
original_quote: unless you start to treat innovation or creativity as a capacity that can be built and nurtured and grown and attended to regularly, you're never going to win the medal and you're often going to pull a muscle.
implication: Leaders should stop hiring for 'creative people' as if creativity is a static trait and instead invest in systems and routines that grow the creative capacity of their entire team over time.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy's book, Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters, centers on a single key metric — the flow of ideas — as the most important measure of organizational and individual creative health. This reframes what companies should be tracking and optimizing.
original_quote: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters
implication: Organizations obsessed with output metrics like revenue or features shipped may be missing the upstream driver of all those outcomes: the rate and quality of ideas being generated. Ideaflow is the leading indicator worth monitoring.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy frames innovation not just as a professional skill but as a means of redemption — coming up with creative solutions that redeem broken parts of the world. This gives innovation a moral and spiritual dimension beyond business performance.
original_quote: come up with creative solutions that redeem broken parts of the world
implication: For faith-driven entrepreneurs especially, innovation is not merely a competitive advantage but a calling — a way of participating in the repair of systems, communities, and structures that are not working as they should.
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy draws a sharp contrast between having a mindset that innovation requires ongoing practice versus those who treat it as something that happens spontaneously or only at designated events. The mindset difference — practice versus event — determines who will consistently produce innovative outcomes.
original_quote: there's not a mindset of you're not. I need to stretch. You know what I need to do training
implication: The most important prerequisite for becoming more innovative is a mindset shift: from 'I'll be creative when inspiration strikes or when we hold a brainstorm' to 'I am actively training my creative capacity every day.'
source_ref: Episode 259 – Practicing Innovation with Jeremy Utley

content: The core insight of Idea Flow is that improving the quality of ideas is the wrong goal. Instead, organizations and individuals should focus on amplifying the quantity of ideas they generate. More ideas in the pipeline statistically increases the likelihood of breakthrough ideas emerging.
implication: Leaders and creators should stop trying to have better ideas and start building systems that produce more ideas. Volume is the lever, not judgment or refinement at the front end.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Idea Flow is a measurable concept — not just a metaphor. Jeremy argues there is an 'easiest way to measure your idea flow,' implying that individuals and organizations can track and monitor their idea output as a concrete metric, much like other performance indicators.
implication: Treat idea generation as a measurable business process. If you can measure it, you can manage it — making creativity a discipline rather than a mystery.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Idea Flow can be deliberately dialed up in the context of skill development. Jeremy discusses specific ways individuals can increase their idea flow when working on developing new capabilities, suggesting that creativity and learning are intertwined processes that can be optimized together.
implication: When building new skills, intentionally increasing your idea generation around that skill accelerates mastery. Creative volume and skill development reinforce each other.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: To change the output of your thinking, you must attend to the inputs. Jeremy emphasizes that the quality and variety of what you consume, encounter, and expose yourself to directly shapes the ideas you are capable of producing. Garbage in, garbage out — but also: novelty in, novelty out.
implication: Creators and leaders should be deliberate curators of their information diet. Changing what you read, watch, and who you talk to is a direct intervention on the quality and originality of your ideas.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Connections between people and ideas are a critical engine of idea generation. Jeremy highlights the importance of connections when generating more ideas, suggesting that creative output is not a solo endeavor but is amplified through networks and relationships.
implication: Building diverse, cross-functional relationships is not just good for culture — it is a direct investment in your idea-generating capacity. Isolated thinkers produce fewer and narrower ideas.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: The inspiration needed to solve organizational problems often lives outside the organization. Jeremy argues that looking inward for creative solutions is a limiting strategy, and that external perspectives, industries, and stimuli are essential sources of fresh ideas.
implication: Organizations should systematically build in exposure to outside industries, customers, and thinkers. Innovation dies when teams only talk to themselves.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Multiple perspectives are a powerful force multiplier in idea creation. Jeremy stresses the importance of having diverse viewpoints when generating ideas, as each additional perspective introduces new angles and combinations that a homogeneous group would never surface.
implication: Homogeneous teams are an idea-generation liability. Leaders who build cognitively and experientially diverse teams gain a structural advantage in creative output.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Embracing low-stakes failures is a prerequisite for large-scale successes. Jeremy frames small failures not as setbacks but as necessary experiments in the idea-generation process, arguing that a tolerance for low-risk failure is what enables breakthrough results over time.
implication: Leaders must create psychological and structural permission for small failures. Teams that cannot fail small will never succeed big — risk-aversion at the micro level kills macro innovation.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Idea Flow is framed as a paradigm shift — a fundamentally different mental model for how creativity works at the enterprise level. Rather than treating idea generation as an episodic creative event, Utley repositions it as a continuous, manageable flow that can be engineered and scaled.
implication: Organizations that treat creativity as an occasional brainstorm session are structurally underinvesting in innovation. Adopting an 'idea flow' operating model means making idea generation an ongoing, embedded practice.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy is actively working on AI projects, indicating he is exploring how artificial intelligence intersects with creativity and idea generation. This positions him at the frontier of applying AI as a tool to enhance or scale human creative output.
implication: AI is not just a productivity tool — forward-thinking creativity researchers like Utley are already investigating how it can serve as a partner in the idea generation process. Leaders should be paying attention to this emerging interface.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Jeremy co-authored the book Idea Flow, which serves as the foundational text for his framework on scaling creative output. The book is specifically oriented toward large-scale enterprises, suggesting the principles are designed to work at organizational rather than just individual scale.
implication: For enterprise leaders seeking to operationalize creativity, Idea Flow offers a research-grounded framework. This is not self-help for individuals — it is a system design manual for organizations.
source_ref: The Science of Idea Flow with Jeremy Utley

content: Feeling behind on AI is the only rational response to the current pace of change. The tools are changing monthly, capabilities weekly, and expectations daily — so the sensation of not keeping up is evidence of paying attention, not evidence of failure.
original_quote: Behind is the only rational response to what's happening right now. The tools are changing monthly. The capabilities are changing weekly. The expectations are changing daily. If you feel like you can't keep up, congratulations — you're paying attention.
implication: Leaders and individuals should stop treating their sense of 'behind-ness' as a personal deficiency and start treating it as a calibration signal — it means they're engaged with reality.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: There are two distinct fears people carry about AI, and they are not the same. Fear of being behind is productive — it identifies a closable gap. Fear of replacement is paralyzing — it points at a threat that feels impossible to neutralize, and leads to disengagement rather than action.
original_quote: Fear of replacement doesn't point at a gap you can close — it points at a threat you can't neutralize. It sounds like: What if it's already better than me? What if I learn all of this and still get displaced? What if leaning in only accelerates my own obsolescence?
implication: Before designing any AI learning program, leaders should diagnose which fear their people are carrying. The interventions needed are fundamentally different depending on the answer.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Fear of replacement is self-fulfilling in a concrete, mechanistic way. The person who believes they will be displaced stops experimenting and learning — and then eventually does fall behind, not because AI replaced them, but because they opted out of learning.
original_quote: This fear is often self-fulfilling. The person who believes they're going to be replaced stops experimenting. Stops learning. Stops being curious. And then, eventually, they do fall behind — not because AI replaced them, but because they replaced themselves. They opted out.
implication: The greatest AI risk for most professionals is not displacement by the technology itself, but self-displacement through avoidance behaviors triggered by replacement anxiety.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: The PACE model — Prediction, Appraisal, Curiosity, Exploration — describes the neurological sequence that must fire for learning to stick. Crucially, the Appraisal step is a threat-safety check: if the brain perceives too high a threat, curiosity never activates, dopamine doesn't fire, and learning doesn't land regardless of effort or exposure.
original_quote: The A is Appraisal — your brain asks a safety question before it lets you get curious. Am I safe here? When curiosity fires, the brain actually turns down the volume on signals associated with risk. But it won't do that until it's assessed the threat level. If the threat is too high, curiosity never gets switched on.
implication: Organizations cannot mandate learning. They must first reduce perceived threat levels so the neurological conditions for curiosity — and therefore real learning — can be met.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Dopamine is not merely a pleasure chemical — it is a neuroplasticity catalyst that allows learning to stick. Without curiosity triggering dopamine, a person can be physically present in a training session and go through all the motions without the learning actually landing.
original_quote: Dopamine is not just a feel-good chemical. Dopamine is a neuroplasticity catalyst. It's what allows the learning to stick. Without curiosity, you can be in the room, you can go through the motions, but the learning doesn't land.
implication: Attendance and compliance metrics are poor proxies for AI skill development. What matters is whether employees are in a neurological state of curiosity when they engage with new tools.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Given identical inputs — same experience, same skills, same background — a person who approaches an AI interaction with curiosity rather than threat will measurably get more benefit from that interaction. Emotional starting point is itself a skill-multiplier.
original_quote: Given the same number of experiences, the same skills, the same background — if you feel curious instead of threatened, the benefit you'll get from each interaction with AI will be higher.
implication: Investing in the emotional and psychological framing around AI adoption is not soft work — it directly determines ROI on every AI training and tooling investment an organization makes.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Every organizational communication about AI — every all-hands, every change management memo, every enthusiastic demo — is an appraisal event. If the framing triggers replacement-fear rather than curiosity, the brain's threat response suppresses the very learning the organization is trying to produce.
original_quote: Every all-hands presentation, every change management communication, every enthusiastic demo from your most AI-forward leader — each of those is an appraisal event. Your people's brains are running a threat-assessment in real time. If the framing triggers replacement-fear rather than curiosity, the dopamine doesn't fire.
implication: Leaders must audit the implicit framing of every AI communication. 'Here's what's possible' and 'here's what's coming for you' are categorically different messages with categorically different neurological and learning outcomes.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Even the most well-intentioned AI enthusiasts in an organization may be the biggest threat-triggers in the room. The more impressive the demo and the more fluent the prompts, the higher the appraisal threat generated in people who aren't yet fluent — potentially suppressing their curiosity and learning.
original_quote: You're probably already enthusiastic about AI. Which means you're probably the most well-intentioned threat-trigger in the room. The more impressive your demo, the more fluent your prompts, the more it demonstrates what's possible — the higher the appraisal threat you may be generating in someone who isn't yet fluent.
implication: AI champions and early adopters need to actively modulate how they demonstrate capability. Leading with vulnerability and process, not just polished outputs, may be more effective at enabling peer adoption.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Andrej Karpathy — co-founder of OpenAI, former AI lead at Tesla, one of the most accomplished programmers alive — publicly described feeling 'more behind than ever' as a programmer in the AI era. Utley uses this as a powerful reframe: if being behind is the condition even for the field's best, it is weather, not character.
original_quote: If Andrej Karpathy feels behind, what does that mean for the rest of us? ... If one of the most accomplished technologists alive feels behind, then behind isn't a character flaw. It's the weather. The question is just what you do about it.
implication: When helping colleagues or teams overcome AI anxiety, citing Karpathy's admission is a practical reframe tool — it externalizes the feeling of 'behind-ness' from personal failing to universal condition.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: The fear of being behind has a direction — it points at a closable gap. Karpathy's framing of AI's challenge as a 'skill issue' is significant: it categorizes the problem as learnable and actionable, not existential. The right move when feeling behind is to start, pick one thing, and run the experiment.
original_quote: Karpathy's word for it was 'skill issue.' In other words: the gap is closable. The behind-ness isn't a verdict. It's an invitation... The feeling of behind — held correctly — is fuel.
implication: Behind-fear, properly reframed, is one of the most powerful motivators available. The goal is not to eliminate this feeling but to channel it toward the smallest possible concrete next action.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: To lower the appraisal threshold and unlock genuine curiosity, Utley recommends starting with genuinely low-stakes AI use cases rather than critical workflows. Small wins reduce perceived threat, which allows the appraisal step to pass, which triggers curiosity and dopamine, which causes learning to actually land.
original_quote: Don't start with the AI tool that will touch your most critical workflow. Start with something genuinely low-stakes — the one that helps you draft an email, plan a trip, brainstorm a title. Get the wins. The wins lower the perceived threat. Lower threat means the appraisal step passes. The appraisal step passing means curiosity fires. Curiosity firing means dopamine. Dopamine means learning actually lands.
implication: AI onboarding programs should be sequenced from low-stakes to high-stakes, not organized by functional importance. The psychological sequencing matters more than the business priority sequencing.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Utley offers a specific high-value prompt for getting real value from AI: describe your role, list your top three weekly activities, and ask the AI to interview you with 3-5 questions before offering suggestions. This approach grounds AI interaction in actual work context rather than generic demos or tutorials.
original_quote: Open ChatGPT or Claude right now and paste this: 'I'm a [your role] at a [your type of company]. Here are the three things I spend most of my time on each week: [list them]. Before giving me any suggestions, interview me — ask me 3-5 questions about my work so you understand what would actually help.'
implication: The most direct path to personal AI value is a contextual, role-specific conversation rather than general tutorials. Getting AI to ask questions before answering produces dramatically more relevant output.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: A competent professional leader contacted Utley for a private one-on-one AI consultation because he was too self-conscious to ask questions in a group setting. The gap in AI knowledge felt like evidence of personal failing rather than a normal response to unprecedented change — illustrating how shame compounds avoidance.
original_quote: The feeling of being behind wasn't just uncomfortable for him. It felt like it said something about him. Like the gap was evidence of a personal failing, not a normal response to an unprecedented rate of change.
implication: Organizations must create psychologically safe AI learning environments — small groups, peer cohorts, or private channels — where people can ask beginner questions without status threat.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: The discovery of new AI capabilities can intensify the feeling of being behind rather than reduce it. Utley frames this as a feature, not a bug: the more you learn, the more you realize what you've been missing, which is a sign of genuine engagement with the technology's frontier.
original_quote: Sometimes the feeling is strongest right after I discover something new AI can do that I didn't know about. The more you learn, the more you realize how much you're missing. It's a feature, not a bug.
implication: People who feel increasingly behind as they learn more about AI are probably on the right track — this is the sensation of expanding awareness, not regression. It should be normalized and even celebrated in learning culture.
source_ref: Not Behind Enough to Panic. Too Afraid to Learn.

content: Most people treat AI like a vending machine — opening a blank chat window and asking a stranger to improvise — rather than onboarding it like a real teammate. The result is generic output, but the fault isn't the AI's capability; it's the absence of context. The fix isn't better prompts; it's treating the AI as a colleague who needs to be properly onboarded.
original_quote: They want a chief of staff, but they keep opening a blank chat window and asking a stranger to improvise. (And then, somehow, the stranger gets blamed for sounding like a stranger.)
implication: Leaders and creators should stop optimizing prompts and start investing in onboarding their AI with real context — who they are, how they sound, what they hate, and what good work looks like.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: A tool needs instructions, but a teammate needs context. This distinction reframes the entire relationship with AI — moving from a transactional exchange of commands to a working collaboration built on shared understanding. The best AI users Jeremy knows aren't writing more elaborate prompts; they're building reusable context around their work.
original_quote: A tool needs instructions. A teammate needs context. That's the shift.
implication: Professionals should invest time once in building a reusable 'working brief' for their AI — covering identity, voice, priorities, and rules — rather than re-explaining themselves in every new session.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: The real bottleneck to effective AI use isn't AI literacy — it's self-literacy. Most people underuse AI because they haven't done the work of making their own context legible: who they are, how they sound, what they refuse, and what they keep explaining repeatedly. AI is simply the first colleague that actually demands this kind of self-knowledge.
original_quote: The bottleneck is not AI literacy. It's self-literacy. We are underusing AI because we have not done the work of making our own context legible.
implication: Before blaming AI tools for poor output, individuals and teams should ask whether they've ever clearly articulated their own voice, values, standards, and working rules — because AI cannot infer what hasn't been examined.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Jeremy used AI to solve a task he'd been avoiding — building an FAQ — by having it read 50 call transcripts, identify recurring questions, and draft answers in his own voice. The AI didn't invent anything; it found the through-line in content he'd already created. This produced the sensation of genuine leverage, not just utility.
original_quote: It read 50 transcripts, found the recurring questions, pulled my actual answers, and wrote the FAQ. In about three minutes... That was one of the first moments where I didn't just think, 'AI is useful.' I thought, 'Oh… This is what it feels like to have leverage.'
implication: Before asking AI to generate new content, creators and professionals should first point it at their existing material — transcripts, emails, notes, past drafts — because the answers often already exist and just need to be surfaced.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: The most underrated category of AI use may be getting it to be 'appropriately boring' — handling the tasks that are easy enough that you feel like you should do them, but annoying enough that you never actually will. This isn't about AI creativity; it's about AI reliability for the mundane-but-important work that keeps piling up.
original_quote: I did not need it to be creative. I needed it to be appropriately boring. (Which, now that I say it, might be an underrated category of AI use.)
implication: High-value professionals should audit their task lists for items they've been avoiding because they're tedious — not hard — and systematically delegate those to AI rather than waiting to use AI only for glamorous or complex work.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: The first AI teammate is the hardest — not because the technology is difficult, but because setting it up forces you to notice yourself. You have to name what matters, write down your rules, listen for your own voice, and decide what good work looks like before you can ask AI to produce it. This self-examination is the real cost and the real payoff.
original_quote: The first AI teammate is the hard one. Not because the technology is impossible. Because the first one forces you to notice yourself.
implication: Leaders should expect the initial investment in AI onboarding to feel uncomfortable precisely because it demands self-examination — and should treat that discomfort as a feature, not a bug, since the resulting clarity compounds across every future AI collaboration.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Once the first AI teammate is properly onboarded with core identity, voice, rules, and context, every subsequent AI teammate becomes dramatically cheaper to spin up — inheriting that foundation and only requiring role-specific additions. This creates compounding leverage rather than a series of one-off interactions.
original_quote: A research teammate can inherit your identity, voice, rules, and current context. You only add the research-specific job... By the fifth one, you are not starting over anymore. That is the deeper leverage.
implication: The ROI of AI onboarding is not linear — it compounds. Investing in a reusable 'Teammate Stack' means each new AI role costs less to configure and delivers faster results, making the upfront work increasingly worthwhile over time.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Jeremy frames the key reorienting question not as 'What prompt should I use?' but as 'What would this AI need to know if it were joining my team?' This question transforms prompting from performance into preparation, and turns the chat window from a vending machine into a working relationship.
original_quote: What would this AI need to know if it were joining my team? That question changes everything. It turns prompting into onboarding. It turns one-off answers into reusable leverage.
implication: Anyone serious about AI productivity should replace their default prompt-crafting ritual with a teammate-onboarding mindset — asking what a smart new hire would need to know on day one, then building that brief once and reusing it.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Jeremy recommends a specific onboarding practice: before any important AI session, have the AI interview you — one question at a time — to build a working brief capturing who you are, how you sound, what you hate, and what the AI should never do. The interview format draws out things you'd say out loud but never type.
original_quote: I want to onboard you as a teammate before I ask you to help me. Please ask me one question at a time, waiting for my answers in between. Help me create a short working brief that captures who I am, what I'm working on, how I sound, what I hate, and what you should never do.
implication: Practitioners can immediately improve AI output quality by running a one-time AI interview session to generate a reusable working brief — replacing the habit of diving straight into task prompts with a brief context-building conversation first.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Jeremy emphasizes that telling AI what you hate — what sounds fake, what risks matter, when to challenge you — is the step most people skip when onboarding. Defining what the AI should never do is as important as defining what it should produce, because it's the guard against generic, inauthentic output.
original_quote: This is the part people skip. Tell it what you hate. Tell it what sounds fake. Tell it what risks matter. Tell it when to challenge you, when to ask, and when to stop polishing and just make the thing useful.
implication: AI onboarding should include explicit negative constraints — a 'never do this' list — because AI defaults to the generic center of the distribution, and only explicit guardrails pull output toward an individual's authentic voice and standards.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: The language we use to describe our relationship with AI reveals our underlying mindset. Asking whether you 'use' AI — as opposed to 'working with' it — parallels the distinction between using a CMO versus working with one. The framing shapes the entire quality of the collaboration.
original_quote: Do you 'use' your CMO? Your legal counsel? No — you work with them. The language we use reveals our mindset.
implication: Organizations and individuals should audit their language around AI — shifting from 'using AI' to 'working with AI' — because the linguistic shift signals and reinforces a fundamentally different set of behaviors, expectations, and collaboration norms.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: AI is a skill, not a pill — and the skill isn't memorizing clever prompts, it's learning how to build the conditions for better collaboration. This means noticing and constructing the context that most people have never bothered to articulate, even for their human colleagues.
original_quote: AI is a skill, not a pill. The skill is not memorizing clever wording. The skill is learning how to build the conditions for better collaboration. Which, unfortunately, means noticing the conditions you've never bothered to build.
implication: AI education and training programs should shift focus from prompt engineering tricks to collaboration infrastructure — teaching people how to articulate their context, voice, and standards in ways that make any collaborative relationship more productive.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Many professionals have a category of tasks on their list that are easy enough they feel they should do them, but annoying enough they never actually will — what Jeremy calls the 'right after I retire' pile. AI is particularly well-suited to exactly this category, not because the tasks are technically complex but because they require assembly of existing material.
original_quote: It was easy enough that I felt like I should do it, and annoying enough that I knew I never actually would do it. Right after I retire, maybe.
implication: A productive AI audit starts not with your hardest problems but with your most-avoided easy ones — the tasks that have lingered on your list due to inertia, not complexity — because those often have the raw material already available and just need an AI to assemble it.
source_ref: Stop Reintroducing Yourself to AI

content: Inspiration is not a passive, mystical event but a disciplined practice of seeking unexpected input. Utley argues that actively and systematically pursuing novel inputs is the foundational method for solving problems. The discipline lies in consistently going out to find what you don't already know.
original_quote: Inspiration is the disciplined pursuit of unexpected input. And every one of those words matters, but being disciplined in your pursuit of input is the way to solve problems.
implication: Leaders and creators should treat inspiration as a repeatable process, not a lucky accident. Build structured routines for consuming unexpected inputs — outside your industry, domain, or comfort zone.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: The most innovative people share a common instinct: when stuck, they go seek external input rather than grinding harder on the problem. This externally-directed reflex is what separates prolific innovators from those who stall. Fresh thinking is downstream of fresh input.
original_quote: The most innovative individuals have this instinct to go and seek input, that drives fresh thinking when they're stuck.
implication: When you hit a creative wall, the productive move is not to push harder but to go looking — for analogies, adjacent solutions, or unexpected perspectives. Train this as a reflex.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Ideaflow reframes the core problem of innovation: the real challenge isn't having good ideas, it's solving the problem of problem-solving itself. By focusing on how you think about a problem — not just the problem itself — ideaflow offers a meta-level solution to creative and business challenges.
original_quote: We believe that idea flow can solve the problem of solving problems for good. Because you realize it's actually about how you think about the problem that matters.
implication: Organizations should invest upstream in how problems are framed and how ideas are generated, not just in evaluating outputs. Improving the thinking process yields compounding returns on innovation.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Most people have a deeply flawed default assumption: that the majority of their ideas are good, commercially viable, and worth pursuing. The reality is the opposite — most ideas are not viable. Recognizing this bias is a prerequisite for building a healthy innovation practice.
original_quote: Our default assumption is to think that the majority of ideas we have are good, commercially viable, and successful. The opposite is true.
implication: Builders and leaders must dismantle the ego-protection around their ideas. Accepting that most ideas will fail creates the psychological permission to generate far more of them — which is the actual path to finding winners.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Solutions to your problems often already exist somewhere in the world — just not in your industry. If you're thoughtful about where you look, you can stumble upon novel solutions that would never appear within your own domain. Cross-industry analogical thinking is a powerful but underused innovation tool.
original_quote: Just because you don't know how to solve a problem doesn't mean it hasn't been solved in the world more broadly. And a lot of times, if you're thoughtful about where you go looking, you stumble upon novel solutions that you never would've seen in your own industry.
implication: Deliberately seek out how other industries, disciplines, or domains have solved structurally similar problems. The solution to your most pressing challenge may already exist — just not where you're looking.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Ideaflow is an input metric, not an output metric. It measures how many ideas you can generate at any moment — the throughput and velocity of idea generation — rather than how many good ideas emerge. Treating it as an output metric misunderstands its function.
original_quote: When you think about idea flow, it's not a measure of how many good ideas you can generate at any moment. That's an output metric. It's a measure of how many ideas you can generate at any moment and how many ideas are being generated.
implication: Organizations and individuals should track and optimize the volume and velocity of idea generation as a leading indicator of innovation health, not just evaluate the quality of final outputs.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: History's greatest innovators — from Benjamin Franklin to Steve Jobs to Frank Lloyd Wright — shared a counterintuitive habit: they regularly walked away from their work. Napping, gardening, and mundane activities weren't distractions but essential parts of their creative process. They created space for inspiration to arrive rather than forcing it.
implication: High-output creators should deliberately schedule downtime and disengagement as part of their innovation practice. Stepping away is not laziness — it is a cognitive strategy for allowing subconscious processing and unexpected connections.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Utley's book Ideaflow proposes a strategy not just for generating more ideas but for determining which ones are worth pursuing. The framework positions idea generation and idea evaluation as distinct, coupled disciplines. Without a method for filtering, high volume alone doesn't produce innovation.
implication: Innovation systems need two engines: one for generating ideas at volume and one for systematically evaluating which to pursue. Building only one without the other is a structural gap most organizations have.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Utley serves as Director of Executive Education at Stanford's d.school (Hasso Plattner Institute of Design), applying design thinking principles to help leaders around the world innovate more effectively. His work bridges academic design methodology and practical leadership application.
implication: Design thinking is not just a product or UX tool — it is a leadership capability. Executives who develop design thinking fluency are better equipped to frame problems, generate options, and navigate uncertainty.
source_ref: Episode 248: Jeremy Utley — unSILOed Podcast

content: Jeremy Utley argues that ideation should never be framed as 'coming up with good ideas.' Introducing quality as a criterion at the outset of ideation is a mistake — quality judgments belong later in the process, not at the beginning. Effective ideation is about generating volume and possibility, not filtering prematurely.
original_quote: You're wrong. Stop. No. Okay. Go. This drives me crazy. Thank you, David, for the opportunity to get on my high horse for a second. Good. There's, why good? Why introduce the concept of quality? And that's what everybody does, right? Everybody says ideation is coming up with good ideas, but the problem is good. And good. I don't think quality has any bearing on the conversation at the beginning.
implication: Leaders and facilitators should explicitly ban quality filters during early ideation. Framing brainstorming as 'find good ideas' kills creative volume before it starts — structure your sessions around quantity and divergence first.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy and his research partner Kian Gohar conducted systematic research into how teams use generative AI for problem solving, motivated by a gap between anecdotal experience and demonstrable evidence. They wanted to move beyond hunches to make empirically grounded claims about AI's impact on team-based ideation. This represents a deliberate shift from practitioner intuition to academic rigor.
original_quote: The question was, what can we demonstrate apart from kind of anecdotal observation and what kinds of claims can we make about the impact of generative AI on the way teams work when they're trying to solve problems.
implication: Practitioners and organizations adopting AI for innovation work should demand evidence-based frameworks rather than relying solely on personal anecdote or vendor claims. The field is young enough that early empirical research carries significant weight.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: When Jeremy first saw the research results come in, his immediate reaction was 'oh no' — a signal he interprets as a good sign for academic research. Surprising or uncomfortable findings that challenge your hypotheses are intellectually valuable. The data revealed something both fascinating and cautionary about how cognitive bias undermines AI-assisted teams.
original_quote: When we first started seeing results flow in, my first thought was, oh no, oh no. You know, and actually it's a good sign as an academic researcher when you get the data back and you think, oh no, because then you wait a few beats. And I remember that first day, Kian and I are on the phone looking at stuff and I go, Oh, yeah.
implication: For researchers and innovators alike, data that surprises or unsettles you is often the most valuable. Build processes that surface uncomfortable results rather than confirming what you already believe.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy's research uncovered a specific cognitive bias that can seriously hamper AI-assisted teams from reaching their full creative potential. Crucially, this bias is described as both predictable and preventable — meaning awareness alone is a powerful antidote. The research frames this as a cautionary tale with a practical remedy.
original_quote: A specific cognitive bias can seriously hamper AI assisted teams from reaching their full potential. Luckily, this bias is as preventable as it is predictable for folks who are aware of the phenomenon.
implication: Organizations deploying AI in creative or problem-solving workflows must invest in cognitive bias training alongside technical onboarding. Knowing the bias exists is already a meaningful protective factor.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy published a book on brainstorming and idea generation one month before ChatGPT launched — an event he describes as the equivalent of writing the world's greatest book on retail before the internet was invented. Rather than defending his prior work, he immediately pivoted: he left his operational responsibilities at Stanford to go all-in on exploring AI's implications for innovation.
original_quote: I wrote what I thought would be the world's greatest book on brainstorming and idea generation. That was published one month before ChatGPT came out, which I consider to be the equivalent of writing the world's greatest book on retail prior to the invention of the internet. The sands upon which my expertise has built are shifting rapidly. So about a month after ChatGPT came out, I left my operational responsibilities at Stanford to go all in exploring the implications of AI on innovation and entrepreneurship and organizations.
implication: Expertise is not a fixed asset — it has a shelf life. Leaders and practitioners must be willing to reassess and rebuild their knowledge base when foundational technologies shift. Jeremy models intellectual honesty and adaptive action over ego protection.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy co-founded Stanford's Masters of Creativity program at the d.school (Hasso Platner Institute of Design), which takes students from all walks of life through activities designed to unlock creative potential. The program explicitly teaches not just how to generate lots of ideas, but how to sort the good ones from the bad ones — a two-stage creative competency. AI tools like ChatGPT are now part of that toolkit.
original_quote: Jeremy Utley co-founded a very popular program there called Stanford's Masters of Creativity. They literally take students from all walks of life, and put them through programs and activities designed to unlock their creative potentials.
implication: Creativity can be systematically taught and developed — it is not a fixed trait. Organizations that invest in structured creativity development alongside AI tool adoption will compound their advantages faster than those who treat either as optional.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy spent 15 years teaching at Stanford's d.school with a specialization in creativity and entrepreneurship. His body of work bridges academic research and organizational practice — he advises organizations on building innovation capacity, not just teaching in a classroom. This practitioner-researcher hybrid role shapes how he approaches AI's impact on creative work.
original_quote: I've spent the last 15 years teaching at Stanford's D school, wrote what I thought would be the world's greatest book on brainstorming and idea generation.
implication: The most valuable AI-and-creativity insights will come from people who operate at the intersection of empirical research and real organizational practice — not purely from either academic or consulting silos.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy's research with Kian Gohar was triggered by questions emerging from their professional networks when ChatGPT launched: Is ideation dead? What are the implications for creativity and innovation? These weren't abstract academic questions — they were urgent practical concerns from organizations trying to figure out how to adapt. The research was explicitly designed to answer real-world organizational questions.
original_quote: Both Kian and I, when ChatGPT came out, we started playing and because of the roles we played advising organizations and building capacity around innovation, we start hearing kind of murmurs in our respective networks around what are the implications for innovation? What are the implications for creativity? Is ideation dead?
implication: The most relevant AI research is being driven by pressing practitioner questions, not just academic curiosity. Organizations should cultivate relationships with researcher-practitioners who are actively embedded in real business contexts.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy and Kian developed a paradigm for getting the most out of team-based AI-assisted ideation, incorporating concepts like the 'Taylor Swift method,' the 'Einstein effect,' specific GPT prompts, and cognitive bias mitigation. The research wasn't just descriptive — it produced actionable, science-based frameworks for practitioners. This represents a rare combination of empirical rigor and practical application.
original_quote: Jeremy and Kian have developed a whole paradigm for getting the most out of team based AI assisted ideation. It involves Taylor Swift, the Einstein effect, GPT prompts, cognitive biases.
implication: Effective AI-assisted ideation is not intuitive — it requires deliberate frameworks. Teams that adopt structured methodologies grounded in behavioral science will consistently outperform those who use AI tools ad hoc.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy hosts the Beyond the Prompt podcast, which focuses on how professionals around the world are using AI tools like ChatGPT in their actual work right now. The show is explicitly oriented toward application over speculation — featuring real companies, real use cases, and lessons on leveraging AI to accelerate business outcomes. This positions Jeremy as a practitioner-communicator bridging research and real-world adoption.
original_quote: Jeremy just launched a new podcast called Beyond the Prompt. The premise is that this is a show about how professionals around the world are using AI tools like ChatGBT at their companies right now, in their work.
implication: The most valuable learning resources on AI adoption are those grounded in current, applied practice rather than theoretical possibility. Practitioners should prioritize sources documenting what is actually working today.
source_ref: New Research: Get Better Ideas with AI

content: Jeremy distinguishes between tasks and problems: tasks have known solutions and are simply executed, while problems are situations where the right path is unclear. For problems, the instinct to reach for a single solution is a trap — what's needed first is a volume of ideas before any evaluation begins.
original_quote: Tasks are straightforward. You know how to do. The answer is apparent. It's just a matter of execution. But a problem is something that you're not exactly sure what the right way to handle it is.
implication: Leaders and teams should resist the urge to converge on a solution immediately. The first move when facing a genuine problem should always be divergence — generating many possibilities before selecting any.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy argues that every problem — by definition — is an idea problem. The moment you recognize you don't know how to proceed, the primary resource you need is not a solution but a flood of possibilities. Volume precedes quality.
original_quote: When you have a problem on your hands, the first thing you need to recognize is what you need is lots of ideas. Problems yield to ideas and we feel relief in the face of a problem when we feel like we have a good idea.
implication: Reframing every unsolved challenge as an 'idea problem' is a mindset shift that unlocks better outcomes. Before asking 'what's the answer?' ask 'how many answers can we generate?'
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy frames Ideaflow as a measurable business metric: ideas over time. Rather than treating creativity as a soft, unquantifiable asset, this framing makes the generation of ideas tractable and improvable — something an organization can actually manage and optimize.
original_quote: the creation of ideas can be summed up as a complex mathematical equation of ideas over time
implication: Organizations that start tracking the volume and velocity of ideas moving through their teams gain a meaningful lever for innovation performance — one that most competitors completely ignore.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy emphasizes that volume must precede evaluation. Labeling ideas as 'good' or 'bad' too early shuts down the generative process. The goal in early ideation is quantity, not quality — quality emerges from a larger pool of raw material.
original_quote: before good or bad, ever enter the picture. First generate a volume of possibilities before evaluating them.
implication: Facilitators and leaders should actively protect the generative phase from premature judgment. Psychological safety to produce 'bad' ideas is a structural prerequisite for eventually finding great ones.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy and Perry teach that unworkable or seemingly silly ideas should not be filtered out during ideation. Even ideas that are ultimately discarded may contain seeds or components that contribute to the final solution. Stifling the flow kills those hidden contributions.
original_quote: To find great ideas, you have to let the unworkable and goofy ideas flow, as well. Don't stifle the process!
implication: Teams need explicit permission — and ideally structured facilitation — to explore ideas that feel wrong or embarrassing. The cost of filtering too early is far higher than the cost of considering too many ideas.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy challenges the myth that idea generation is a solo activity. Collaborative ideation draws on a wider pool of perspectives, backgrounds, and experiences, producing ideas that no single individual could reach alone. Diverse input expands the solution space.
original_quote: creating ideas with a team can create broader insights, by drawing on a wider pool of perspectives and backgrounds to create ideas that might be outside the experience of a single person.
implication: Building diverse, cross-functional ideation teams is not just a cultural good — it's a direct multiplier on the quality and range of ideas an organization can generate.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy and Perry warn against locking into solutions too early, even when something appears to be working. Early-stage success can create a false sense of certainty that prematurely closes off exploration of potentially superior alternatives.
original_quote: In the early stages of business, resist the urge to 'lock in' on something just because it is working. Continue to generate ideas and innovate.
implication: Early traction is a signal to keep exploring, not to stop. Founders and leaders should treat initial success as validation of the space, not as permission to stop generating alternatives.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy advocates for expanding one's professional network beyond a small recurring team as a practical habit for becoming a better idea generator. Repeated collaboration with the same small group limits the diversity of inputs that fuel novel thinking.
