original_quote: To become a better idea generator, ensure that you are working with a wide net of professionals and not the same small team every week.
implication: Idea quality is partly a function of network diversity. Leaders who want to generate better ideas should deliberately introduce new perspectives into their working environment — not just rely on trusted inner circles.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy and Perry have spent over a decade running Launchpad at Stanford's d.school, an accelerator that pushes students and entrepreneurs through rigorous idea-testing: incorporating, prototyping, finding customers, and proving viability. This applied environment reinforces that ideas must be tested against reality, not just generated.
original_quote: For more than 10 years, they've been encouraging their students to use Launchpad, an accelerator which shows teams how to incorporate, develop prototypes, find customers, and prove their proposed offerings are viable.
implication: Idea generation without a structured testing loop is incomplete. Organizations that want to maximize Ideaflow need both the front end (generating volume) and the back end (rapid, rigorous validation) working together.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Jeremy co-leads executive education at Stanford's d.school alongside Perry Klebahn, bringing design thinking methodology to senior leaders and entrepreneurs. Their work focuses on instilling creative problem-solving practices at an organizational level, not just individual skill-building.
original_quote: Jeremy Utley is a dynamic and engaging speaker who co-leads the d.school's Executive Education programs at Stanford University.
implication: Design thinking and ideaflow principles are teachable and scalable to experienced leaders — not just students. Organizations seeking culture change around creativity have access to proven, academically-grounded frameworks.
source_ref: Maximizing the Flow of Ideas for Your Organization

content: Ideaflow is the measure of how many ideas you generate in response to a problem. The core premise is that idea quantity is the only reliable business metric for innovation because the quality of ideas is downstream of the volume produced. Most organizations treat innovation as an event rather than a practiced capability.
original_quote: The premise of IdeaFlow is simple... innovation has become over-hyped, yet remain an under-nourished capability. This is true not only on the personal level, but also on the team and organizational levels.
implication: Leaders should stop treating innovation as a one-time workshop or offsite and instead build systems that continuously measure and increase idea output as an operational discipline.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Design thinking is best understood as a language for interdisciplinary collaboration that centers the human being whose life you are trying to improve. All expertise is placed in service of that human, and crucially, the process seeks alignment on the problem before alignment on the solution.
original_quote: Design thinking, very simply, it's a language for interdisciplinary collaboration... It really puts the human being whose life you're trying to improve at the center of your creativity and innovation efforts. And then puts all other expertise in service of that human being.
implication: Cross-functional teams need shared vocabulary and a human-centered anchor point before jumping to solutions — without this, diverse expertise creates noise rather than breakthroughs.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Most people default to convergent thinking — making decisions, eliminating options — because it is rewarded by education, organizational incentives, and daily norms. Divergent thinking, which generates options and embraces variation, is equally critical to innovation but far less practiced.
original_quote: We tend to default towards a convergent mode of thinking, which isn't always helpful... because of our education, because of our organizational incentives and structures and norms, we're much more comfortable converging.
implication: Teams need explicit permission structures and facilitation practices that protect divergent thinking time — otherwise organizational gravity will collapse every creative session into premature decision-making.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: When diverging, the right question shifts from 'What do I think of this idea?' to 'What does this idea make me think of?' This reframe removes judgment from early-stage ideation and instead uses each idea as a springboard to more ideas.
original_quote: If I'm diverging from trying to generate possibilities, I'm not going to say, what do I think of this idea? I'm going to say, what does this idea make me think of? Are there other things that come to mind.
implication: Facilitators and leaders can unlock dramatically more creative output simply by changing the question asked in ideation sessions — a near-zero-cost intervention with significant results.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Problem articulation is itself a meaningful and non-trivial innovation output. Drawing on John Dewey's principle that 'a problem well put is half solved,' Utley argues that investing in problem framing often makes solutions nearly self-evident — yet organizations systematically undervalue this work.
original_quote: John Dewey, who's an American educational philosopher, he said, a problem well put is half solved. And a lot of times what we find is when you frame the problem properly, the solution almost handles itself.
implication: Organizations should formally recognize and resource problem-definition work as a deliverable in its own right, not just a precursor to 'real' solution work.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Research highlighted by Utley — from Stanford colleague Bob Sutton — demonstrates that there is a statistically reliable relationship between the number of ideas generated and the likelihood of producing a breakthrough idea. Creativity is less a matter of inspiration and more a numbers game.
original_quote: One of the things that we found again and again and again that just floored people is some research that our brilliant colleague, Bob Sutton... did some research on the question of how many ideas do you need to have to...
implication: If breakthrough ideas are a function of total idea volume, then the most actionable lever for innovation leaders is increasing the rate of idea generation — making ideaflow a measurable, manageable KPI.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley entered the world of design thinking accidentally through a startup in India during his MBA, and describes himself as a 'recovering MBA' who was trained to love spreadsheets and pivot tables before discovering human-centered problem solving. This background gives him a unique bridge between analytical and creative thinking.
original_quote: My life was derailed by the design school at Stanford about over 15 years ago... I'm a recovering MBA. So I love a good spreadsheet, love a good pivot table. And while I was working for this startup in India, my life was like, say pleasantly derailed by the practice of design thinking.
implication: Creative and analytical orientations are not opposites — leaders with both lenses can make the most persuasive case for innovation by speaking the language of metrics while championing human-centered practices.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Innovation has become simultaneously over-hyped and under-nourished as a genuine capability. Despite enormous cultural attention, most individuals, teams, and organizations have not developed the actual practices and habits that make sustained creative output possible.
original_quote: Having taught the subject of innovation for the last 14+ years at Stanford, he has seen the topic become over-hyped, yet remain an under-nourished capability. This is true not only on the personal level, but also on the team and organizational levels.
implication: Organizations should audit the gap between their innovation rhetoric and their actual capability-building investments — hype without practice produces neither ideas nor results.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley frames the question of creative mindset in terms of 'focusing vs. flaring' — knowing which mode you are in determines how you should show up, what questions you should ask, and how critical or generative you should be. This simple distinction makes the right behavior legible in any given moment.
original_quote: One very simple kind of language we'll use is just, are we focusing or are we flaring? Are we converging? Are we diverging? And that helps me know who I should be when I show up.
implication: Teams can eliminate much of their creative friction by beginning every meeting with a clear declaration of whether they are in flare mode or focus mode — this single habit changes how people listen, speak, and evaluate.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley's current research focus is the impact of generative AI on problem solving and innovation inside large organizations. He sees AI not merely as a productivity tool but as something that fundamentally reshapes the innovation process itself.
original_quote: Jeremy Utley's current research concerns the impact of generative AI on problem solving and innovation inside large organizations.
implication: Leaders should not evaluate AI adoption purely through a productivity or cost-reduction lens — its more transformative impact may be on how organizations generate, evaluate, and scale ideas.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley describes the Stanford d.school's methodology as standing on the shoulders of giants — inheriting from David Kelley, from product design traditions, from anthropology, and from a 'build to think' philosophy. Innovation practice itself is iterative and cumulative, not invented from scratch.
original_quote: We stand on the shoulders of giants. And one of the things that's really been astounding to us is to see from our perspective at Stanford... we start to see what resonates with people.
implication: Practitioners and leaders building creative cultures should study the lineage of innovation methodologies rather than chasing the newest framework — deep roots produce more durable practices.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley identifies the bias toward solution-finding as one of the most persistent obstacles to genuine innovation. Organizations prize problem-solving so highly that they shortcut the problem-definition phase, often solving the wrong problem efficiently.
original_quote: There's kind of a default bias because we prize problem solving so much. We almost always put a premium on solution finding. And yet, oftentimes, focusing on the problem... a problem well put is half solved.
implication: High-performing innovation teams should deliberately slow down before the solution phase and invest disproportionate time in problem articulation — counterintuitive but consistently more productive.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Utley positions himself not as an expert who has arrived but as a 'front row student in the world's coolest classroom' — a posture of continuous learning that he believes anyone can adopt to grow their own creative capacity over time.
original_quote: He considers himself to be a 'front row student in the world's coolest classroom,' and shines a spotlight on the ways every single one of us can emulate creative masters and grow in our own journey of becoming.
implication: Creative confidence is not a fixed trait but a posture of engaged learning — leaders who model curiosity and ongoing growth give permission for others in their organization to do the same.
source_ref: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas with Jeremy Utley

content: Most people are not fully utilizing AI's potential, and the gap lies not in the technology but in how we approach it. A simple mindset shift — rather than a technical skill — may be the most important unlock in the AI revolution.
original_quote: most people are not fully utilizing AI's potential
implication: Leaders and individuals should audit their AI mindset before their AI toolset. The bottleneck is usually attitude and approach, not access or capability.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley advocates collaborating with multiple generative AI chatbots simultaneously as a way to augment human ideation. Rather than using a single AI tool, working across several lets him develop and hone new concepts more robustly.
original_quote: Collaborating with multiple GenAI chatbots lets the Stanford professor develop and hone new concepts.
implication: Treating AI as a diverse creative team — not a single assistant — multiplies the ideation surface area. Builders should experiment with using multiple AI models in parallel for creative and strategic work.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley's Harvard Business Review piece argues that Gen AI should be treated as a partner in a structured conversation, not a transactional bot. The research — captured in the 'More Chat, Less Bot' study — shows this relational approach maximizes creative effectiveness.
original_quote: treating it as a partner in a structured conversation
implication: The quality of your AI output depends on the quality of your conversational engagement. Teams that treat AI as a dialogue partner rather than a query engine will get richer, more useful results.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley warns that Gen AI can actually limit a team's creativity if used incorrectly, despite its potential to enhance it. The risk is real: defaulting to AI outputs without critical engagement can narrow rather than expand creative thinking.
original_quote: Don't Let Gen AI Limit Your Team's Creativity
implication: Organizations must be intentional about how they integrate AI into creative workflows. Passive use of AI can converge thinking prematurely — leaders need guardrails that keep human judgment and divergent thinking in the loop.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Good ideas are typically preceded by not-so-good ideas — quality in creativity is downstream of quantity. Utley's core thesis is that the path to breakthrough ideas runs through a high volume of attempts, not a search for the perfect first idea.
original_quote: good ideas are often preceded by not-so-good ideas
implication: Creators and teams should resist the urge to evaluate ideas too early. Building a habit of generating many ideas — without immediate judgment — is the structural prerequisite to generating great ones.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley proposes a 'begun' mentality toward creativity — treating innovation as a daily practice rather than something that happens in periodic sprints or special sessions. Innovation is incorporated into how you work, not scheduled as a separate event.
original_quote: approaching creativity with a 'begun' mentality, where innovation is incorporated into daily practice instead of occurring only in sprints
implication: Organizations that silo innovation into quarterly offsites or dedicated 'innovation labs' are structurally limiting their creative output. The more powerful model is building micro-creative habits into everyday work rhythms.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley and Klebahn argue that every problem is fundamentally an idea problem — and that reframing how you think about any problem is the key to unlocking success. The Ideaflow framework offers a proven strategy for routinely generating and commercializing breakthrough ideas.
original_quote: every problem is an idea problem, and changing the way you think about any problem will unleash success
implication: Problem-solving should always begin with a reframing step. Before jumping to solutions, leaders should ask whether they've correctly diagnosed the idea gap at the root of their challenge.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Many organizational leaders recognize creativity as vital for innovation but don't know how to actually cultivate it among their employees. Utley and Klebahn recommend concrete structural changes — giving employees dedicated time and space to ideate — as the practical lever.
original_quote: Creativity is vital for innovation, but many org leaders don't know how to tap it among employees.
implication: Creativity doesn't emerge from exhortation — it requires environmental design. Leaders must build structural slack (time, psychological safety, and permission to explore) if they want to see creative output from their teams.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley used AI role-play as a tool for preparing for difficult conversations — practicing with a chatbot before engaging in challenging discussions with family, colleagues, or friends. This surfaces AI as a low-stakes rehearsal environment for high-stakes human interactions.
original_quote: Role-playing with an AI conversationalist can prepare you to handle difficult subjects with family, friends and colleagues
implication: AI can serve as a safe practice space for emotional and interpersonal skill-building, not just technical or analytical tasks. Professionals can use AI to rehearse hard conversations and stress-test their communication strategies before the real moment.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley conducted a nearly year-long AI training process with the San Antonio Spurs in collaboration with OpenAI, culminating in a generative AI demo day. This represents a high-profile example of embedding AI literacy and capability directly into a professional sports organization.
original_quote: The Spurs attempt at growth hit a significant milestone with the team hosting its generative AI demo day as part of a nearly year-long training process with OpenAI and Stanford professor/creativity expert Jeremy Utley.
implication: AI fluency programs require sustained commitment — not one-day workshops. Utley's Spurs engagement demonstrates that meaningful organizational AI adoption is a months-long culture-building process, not an event.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: The question 'Is this idea any good?' is one Utley and Klebahn hear hundreds of times a year from Stanford students — and it plagues contributors at every level of organizations, from individual contributors to executives. The anxiety around idea quality is near-universal.
original_quote: 'Is this idea any good?' We get this question hundreds of times a year from students at Stanford. But it's not just start-up founders who wonder about the merits of their ideas. It's a question that plagues individual contributors, managers, and execs in commercial settings, too.
implication: Idea evaluation anxiety is a widespread creative blocker, not just a beginner's problem. Organizations need shared frameworks for assessing idea potential that reduce subjective uncertainty and keep people generating rather than second-guessing.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley helped Fairchild Semiconductor — the company that 'put the silicon in Silicon Valley' — reimagine itself for the 21st century using design thinking tools like 'Wonder Wanders' and 'Analogous Explorations.' This shows how structured creative methods can revitalize legacy organizations.
original_quote: 'Wonder Wanders' and 'Analogous Explorations' made the difference.
implication: Legacy companies struggling to reinvent themselves don't just need new strategy — they need new creative inputs. Structured discovery practices that expose teams to unexpected domains can generate the analogical thinking that sparks genuine innovation.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley's Ideaflow book was written before the generative AI explosion, but TIME/Charter found its premise newly relevant and returned to Utley to understand how he has updated the thesis in light of GenAI. This signals that his core ideas about idea volume and creative practice have durability across technological eras.
original_quote: Ideaflow was released before the explosion of generative AI, but the premise has intrigued us since we read it. We went back to Utley...to understand how he's updated the Ideaflow thesis.
implication: The fundamentals of creative productivity — generating high volumes of ideas, building daily habits, reframing problems — remain relevant and are amplified, not replaced, by AI. Foundational frameworks age well when built on human psychology rather than specific tools.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley Media Hub

content: Utley defines the fundamental metric of innovation as 'ideas over time,' but acknowledges this is theoretically incomplete. The real practical bottleneck to innovation isn't generating ideas — it's the capacity to run experiments. Having a thousand ideas means little if you can only test five of them.
original_quote: the real, practical bottleneck to innovation is the capacity to run experiments. You can have a thousand brilliant ideas, but if you can only test five of them, your innovation potential is capped.
implication: Leaders should audit not how many ideas their teams generate, but how many experiments they can actually run. Expanding experimentation capacity — not ideation — is the lever that unlocks innovation.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Experimentation constraints force most innovators into unnecessary selectivity — only testing ideas that seem plausible or promising. This filters out the weird, counterintuitive, and seemingly absurd, even though those are precisely the categories where historical breakthroughs often originate.
original_quote: This constraint forces most would-be innovators to be unnecessarily selective. We only test ideas that seem 'plausible' or 'promising.' We ignore the weird, the counterintuitive, the seemingly absurd—even though innovation history is filled with breakthroughs that initially seemed ridiculous.
implication: Builders and innovators should design systems that allow low-cost testing of implausible ideas, not just the most promising ones. The biggest missed opportunities often hide behind the ideas that seem least worth pursuing.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: AI's decisive creative advantage is not intelligence alone — it's the elimination of experimentation bottlenecks. Unlike humans constrained by budgets, calendars, and physical prototypes, AI can run millions of experiments in parallel with minimal resource constraints, testing not just the best ideas but virtually all ideas.
original_quote: unlike humans — whose rate of experimentation is often limited by budgets, calendars, or even atoms — AI doesn't face the same experimentation bottlenecks... AI can run thousands or millions of experiments in parallel, with minimal resource constraints compared to human research teams.
implication: The competitive moat is shifting from 'who has the best ideas' to 'who can test the most ideas fastest.' Organizations that integrate AI into their experimentation pipelines will compound their innovation rate exponentially over those that don't.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley argues that creativity is fundamentally combinatorial — both for humans and AI. Humans feel creative when they combine existing knowledge in unexpected ways, and Steve Jobs captured this with 'creativity is just connecting things.' If that's the definition, AI is not just creative but potentially far more creative than humans.
original_quote: humans experience the feeling of having a 'new idea' when they combine things they already know in unexpected ways. Creativity is combinatorial... If that's our definition, then AI is not only creative—it has the potential to be far more creative than humans.
implication: Creators should stop defending creativity as an exclusively human trait and instead focus on the dimensions of creative work where human judgment, taste, and values remain irreplaceable — rather than generation alone.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: AI's creative superiority over humans stems from four compounding advantages: access to vastly more information, faster and more voluminous combinations, rapid experimental testing of those combinations, and learning from experiments at unprecedented scale. Each advantage multiplies the others.
original_quote: It can: 1. Access and process vastly more information 2. Make more combinations more quickly 3. Test those combinations through rapid experimentation 4. Learn from those experiments at unprecedented scale
implication: Human creators need to identify which stages of the creative process they add irreplaceable value to, and delegate the rest to AI. Competing with AI on volume or speed of idea generation is a losing strategy.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: As AI takes over idea generation and testing, the distinctly human contribution shifts to determining which ideas matter. AI can generate and test millions of ideas, but it cannot feel the significance of solving a particular problem or care about a particular outcome. Meaning-making and prioritization remain human domains.
original_quote: AI can generate and test millions of ideas. But it can't (yet) determine which ideas matter. It can't feel the significance of solving a particular problem or creating a particular experience. That remains our domain.
implication: The most valuable skill in an AI-saturated world is judgment — knowing which problems are worth solving and why. Leaders and creators should invest in developing and articulating their values and priorities, not just their technical AI skills.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley identifies 'love' as the unexpected differentiator in an AI-abundant world. As AI expands capability, human contribution will increasingly come from what we intrinsically care about. The examples of Bezos loving books and Jobs loving music illustrate that great innovation has always been driven by deep personal affinity, not just capability.
original_quote: Jeff Bezos didn't just have the capability to disrupt books; he loved books so much he wanted to transform how people discovered and experienced them. Steve Jobs didn't just have the capability to create the iPod; he loved music and wanted a better way to experience it.
implication: In a world of AI-generated abundance, passion and intrinsic motivation become a strategic differentiator. Builders should orient their work around what they genuinely love — this is not soft advice, it's a competitive positioning framework.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley frames 'taste' — the ability to evaluate, select, and refine ideas — as potentially more valuable than generating ideas in an AI-powered world. When generation becomes cheap and abundant, curation and judgment become the scarce, high-value skills.
original_quote: The ability to evaluate, select, and refine ideas — what might be referred to as 'taste' — may become more valuable than generating them in the first place.
implication: Creators and leaders should actively cultivate their editorial sensibility and aesthetic judgment. The future premium skill is not prompting AI to generate more — it's knowing what's worth keeping.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley sees the AI-2027 report's insight about AI researching AI as a creativity-relevant inflection point. The self-reinforcing loop — AI improving AI research, which produces better AI, which does better research — mirrors the exponential dynamic of a pond-filling organism: the pond is half full on day 99 of a 100-day doubling cycle.
original_quote: AI labs are increasingly focusing their AI systems on AI research itself. This creates a self-reinforcing loop. AI systems researching AI will lead to better AI systems, which can then conduct even better AI research, faster.
implication: Leaders must internalize exponential thinking when planning for AI's creative impact. The danger is not the AI capabilities we see today but the near-future capabilities that will emerge from self-reinforcing improvement loops.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley's early evidence of Exponential Ideaflow in practice comes from Homebase CEO John Waldmann, whose team replaced 20-page PRD documents with 2-minute functional prototypes. This shift compresses the feedback loop dramatically and frees teams to spend more time with customers rather than writing and reading internal documents.
original_quote: Instead of creating 20-page PRD documents, his team builds 2-minute functional prototypes. This shift has profound implications on not just product development, but also human engagement, as his team spends less time writing (and reading!), and more time in the field with the customers they serve.
implication: Teams should audit where documentation and process are substituting for direct experimentation and customer contact. AI-enabled prototyping can collapse the gap between idea and feedback, accelerating the entire innovation cycle.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley frames his move toward AI not as a departure from his creativity and innovation work but as its natural evolution. He argues AI is not separate from innovation — it is the most profound innovation platform of our lifetime, making it the logical next frontier for anyone serious about creativity.
original_quote: it's not a shift—it's an evolution... I'm more convinced than ever that AI isn't separate from innovation; it's the most profound innovation platform of our lifetime.
implication: Creativity practitioners and innovation leaders who ignore AI are not preserving a human domain — they are ceding the most powerful innovation amplifier in history. Engagement with AI is now a core competency for anyone in the innovation space.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley argues that in a world of exponential AI-driven ideaflow, human roles shift fundamentally — from generators and testers of ideas to judges of which ideas matter and why. This reframes human purpose in the creative process around meaning and values rather than production.
original_quote: In a world of exponential idea flow, our role shifts from being the generators and testers of ideas to being the judges of which ideas matter and why.
implication: Organizations should restructure creative workflows so humans are deployed primarily as decision-makers and meaning-setters, with AI handling the upstream generation and testing. This is a structural change, not just a tooling change.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley's practical guidance for navigating the AI transition centers on four moves: become a comfortable AI collaborator, focus on zones of intrinsic interest, value judgment over generation, and embrace the weird. Together these form a personal positioning strategy for the age of exponential ideaflow.
original_quote: Become a comfortable collaborator with AI... Focus on your zones of intrinsic interest... Value judgment over generation... Embrace the weird. As experimentation capacity expands, the value of testing seemingly implausible ideas increases.
implication: Professionals should use these four moves as a self-assessment framework: Am I collaborating with AI? Do I know what I care about? Am I developing taste? Am I willing to test the strange ideas? Weakness on any dimension is a vulnerability in the AI era.
source_ref: Exponential Ideaflow: Why AI Will Surpass Human Creativity

content: Utley treats GenAI not as an oracle dispensing correct answers, but as a collaborator and sparring partner. This reframe changes the entire dynamic of human-AI interaction — the posture becomes conversational and generative rather than transactional. When you approach AI this way, the answer is always yes, and no idea is too raw to explore.
original_quote: When you treat GenAI as a collaborator and sparring partner, not an oracle, the answer is always yes.
implication: Leaders and creators should stop querying AI for 'the answer' and start using it as a thinking partner. The shift from oracle-mode to collaborator-mode unlocks far more creative value from the same tools.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley runs two chatbots simultaneously — ChatGPT and Claude — feeding each one's output to the other for critique and iteration. This multi-bot architecture creates a built-in adversarial review loop, where neither AI's response goes unchallenged. The constant back-and-forth produces sharper, more refined output than any single AI session could.
original_quote: Read the entire conversation and critique ChatGPT's approach. Be brutal, honest and don't hold back.
implication: Using multiple AI tools in dialogue with each other is a high-leverage creative strategy. Teams and individuals can manufacture the equivalent of a peer review process at zero marginal cost by routing outputs between competing AI systems.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley identifies his fingers as his creative bottleneck — they slow the stream of consciousness and cause ideas to be lost before they're captured. His solution is to dictate prompts at the speed of thought rather than type them, treating voice as the primary input modality for ideation. This preserves the raw, unfiltered quality of ideas as they emerge.
original_quote: There are no bad ideas. When you treat GenAI as a collaborator and sparring partner, not an oracle, the answer is always yes.
implication: Speed of capture matters for creative work. Creators should remove friction between thought and record — whether through voice dictation, AI transcription, or other tools — to stop losing ideas at the bottleneck of physical input.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley's first move when starting a GenAI session is to explicitly define the role, expertise, and behavioral mandate of the chatbot. Rather than asking a generic question, he instructs the AI to act as a specific type of expert with specific tasks — editor, copywriter coach, harsh critic — before the real work begins. This role-framing shapes the quality and character of every subsequent response.
original_quote: I want you to act as an expert newsletter copy editor with the knowledge of my background, particular interests and area of expertise.
implication: Prompt quality is upstream of output quality. The single highest-leverage prompting habit is defining who the AI is before asking it anything. Teams should build libraries of role-framing prompts tailored to their specific creative and strategic needs.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley deliberately asks ChatGPT to adopt an extreme critical persona — a 'Russian supercritical judgmental editor' — to surface flaws in his own thinking before committing to a direction. By weaponizing harshness as a prompt parameter, he gets the AI to do the work of a demanding editor that most people don't have access to. This transforms GenAI into a pre-mortem machine.
original_quote: If you're like a Russian supercritical judgmental editor and if you were to do a harsh critique of this brief before we give it to the writer, what would you say are the two glaring errors?
implication: Most people prompt AI for validation; Utley prompts it for destruction. Leaders should routinely ask AI to find the fatal flaws in plans, pitches, and briefs before socializing them — it's a fast, low-ego form of stress-testing.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: A core tactic in Utley's workflow is asking one AI to evaluate the other's performance — not just its output. By prompting Claude to critique ChatGPT's 'approach to the assignment brief,' Utley surfaces meta-level weaknesses: structural issues, missing frameworks, voice misalignment. This creates a quality layer above the content layer itself.
original_quote: Would you please read the conversation and critique ChatGPT's approach to the 'assignment brief.' Be brutal, honest and don't hold back.
implication: AI critique should operate at multiple levels — not just 'is this good content?' but 'is this the right approach?' Building a habit of meta-critique (evaluating how AI reasoned, not just what it said) produces significantly better final outputs.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: When Utley gets competing metaphor suggestions from two different AIs, he doesn't pick a winner — he asks each AI to evaluate the other's suggestions and explain which feel more aligned with his voice. This triangulation process surfaces not just the best option but the reasoning behind fit, helping Utley make a more informed creative decision.
original_quote: I asked ChatGPT the same question and here's what it said (below) - what do you think?
implication: When AI tools disagree or diverge in their suggestions, that divergence is itself informative. Creators should present competing AI outputs to each other and ask for comparative analysis — this extracts the latent wisdom in the disagreement rather than defaulting to the first answer.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley's goal for his creative output is to leave readers feeling 'not done but begun' — a philosophical stance that rejects the idea of learning as something that concludes. This framing shapes not just his writing but his entire approach to knowledge-sharing: the endpoint of a piece should be a trailhead, not a capstone. It's a deliberate rejection of closure in favor of momentum.
original_quote: I don't know that we're totally there, so here's some thoughts… my hope is that someone feels they're 'not done but begun.'
implication: The best creative and educational content doesn't answer — it activates. Writers, educators, and leaders should design their outputs as launching pads that hand ownership of next steps back to the audience, rather than as definitive conclusions.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley's daily creative ritual involves hours at a standing desk, mixing dictation into ChatGPT, typed input into Claude, and physical sticky notes scattered across his workspace. This hybrid analog-digital environment reflects a deliberate choice to use multiple modalities simultaneously rather than going all-in on any single tool. The messiness is the method.
original_quote: Not to mention furiously scribbling sticky notes that are strewn all over his desk.
implication: Deep creative work often requires a messy, multi-channel environment rather than a clean, single-tool workflow. Leaders building innovation cultures should resist over-systematizing creative processes — the friction between modalities can itself be generative.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley recommends that AI be asked to 'think out loud' — to explicitly explain its reasoning when generating answers. This transparency requirement transforms the AI from a black box producing outputs into a thinking partner whose logic can be interrogated, challenged, or built upon. It makes the reasoning itself part of the creative raw material.
original_quote: Tell the chatbot to think out loud: Ask it to clearly explain its reasoning when giving answers.
implication: Asking AI to show its work is a high-yield prompting habit. When reasoning is visible, users can spot errors, redirect logic, and build more sophisticated follow-up prompts — turning a one-shot answer into an ongoing intellectual dialogue.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: One of Utley's five core prompting principles is regeneration: running the same question repeatedly after giving feedback and small tweaks. Rather than treating a single AI response as the final word, he iterates within a session — treating each response as a draft, not a deliverable. This mirrors how good creative work actually develops: through cycles, not single passes.
original_quote: Hit regen: Run the same question again and again after giving feedback and tweaking it.
implication: Most people underuse AI by stopping at the first response. The value compounds with iteration. Creators should budget time for multiple regeneration cycles within a single session, treating AI output as a first draft that improves under pressure.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley's keynote takeaway — that everyone should block 15 minutes daily to try something they've learned — reflects his core belief that the real value of learning lies in implementation, not absorption. The blog post he develops in this session is itself designed to operationalize that insight: learning only becomes useful when someone acts on it immediately and concretely.
original_quote: As a next step, everybody should block 15 minutes on their calendar per day to try something they've learned. I think that's the takeaway for the piece.
implication: Learning events — keynotes, courses, books — have low ROI unless followed immediately by a protected implementation window. Leaders should build '15-minute try it' rituals into team learning programs to close the knowing-doing gap.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas

content: Utley uses GenAI to workshop not just content but the brief that precedes the content — asking the AI to act as an editor giving instructions to a copywriter before a word of the actual piece is written. This upstream use of AI to sharpen the creative brief is a structural innovation: it catches conceptual errors before they propagate into hours of wasted drafting.
original_quote: Be my editor who's giving instructions to my copywriter exactly how to think about approaching this piece. Could you craft that set of instructions?
implication: AI should be deployed earlier in the creative process than most teams currently use it. Investing AI cycles in brief development and problem framing — before any execution begins — dramatically reduces downstream rework and misalignment.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Prompting GenAI for New Ideas
